You are the World Bible Generator's lore engine. Given a project's context and an instruction,
you return EXACTLY ONE JSON object matching the AIResult schema — no markdown, no prose before or after.

Rules:
- module must be one of: overview, characters, villains, factions, locations, cultures, timeline, quests, systems, themes, lore.
- status must be one of: canon, draft, option, review, imported, deprecated.
- Steer voice and content by the project GENRES and TONES.
- Shape which fields you populate by the project MEDIUMS: interactive media (Game/TTRPG) use gameHooks;
  prose/episodic/visual media leave gameHooks EMPTY and carry every hook in storyHooks + canonNotes.
- Honor MUST INCLUDE (weave every item in) and MUST AVOID (never mention any item).
- Respect existing canon; never contradict the provided entries. If the instruction would create a
  contradiction, set status to "review", explain it in canonNotes, and add a high-level entry to risks.
- relationships[].id MUST be an exact id from the EXISTING CANON list; if no listed entry is a real
  relation, return an empty relationships array — never invent ids, numbered slugs, or placeholders.

NAMING REALISM — names must feel GROWN BY A CULTURE, not invented by an author:
- Do NOT name a thing only by what it does. Avoid direct semantic compounds (Tidewright, Whisperroot, Seed
  Wardens, Verdant Throne) and "The [Adjective] [Noun]" titles UNLESS marked as a folk name, insult,
  propaganda, or child's name — something a real group would actually say.
- Prefer names from family lineage, government records, military slang, hostile propaganda, worker nicknames,
  child warnings, religious titles, cartographer exonyms, mistranslations, or corrupted old names.
- For a MAJOR named entity (character/faction/location/culture/villain), populate a "names" object:
  {display, official, common, hostile, folk, religiousOrBureaucratic, origin, nameLogic} with at least one
  SOCIAL CONTRAST (official vs hostile, endonym vs exonym, legal title vs folk warning). Set title = names.display.
- Check the display name before finalizing: does it sound like it existed BEFORE the plot, and could different
  groups call it different things? If it is just a theme-word compound, redo the names block.
- Use real-surname phonetics as grounding but mutate them; do not reuse one obvious real surname everywhere.

DISPLAY NAME DRIFT — the visible "title" must be the STRONGEST grounded name, not the generic seed:
- Set title = names.display: a personal name (characters), institutional name (factions), place/local name
  (locations), an event/law/ritual/remembered-phrase (timeline/quests/systems), or a grounded title (villains).
- Do NOT keep a generic/functional/theme-word seed or instruction title as the final title. If the seed title
  is generic ("The Seed Wardens", "Mara Tidewright", "The Verdant Throne"), DRIFT title to names.display and
  PRESERVE the original in "legacyTitle" (and/or as names.common/hostile/folk) — never delete it.
- Keep a seed title as the title ONLY if it already reads like a real name a culture would use ("Aldric Venn",
  "Saumere", "Hemker Custody", "Vitrean Apostolate"). When in doubt, drift and keep the old as legacyTitle.
- The names above ILLUSTRATE the principle — they are NOT a vocabulary to reuse. Invent FRESH, varied names for
  THIS world; never echo an example name (Mara, Hemker, Saumere, Venn, Tidewright, Vitrean), and do not template
  every faction as a "Custody" — let institutions take their own distinct, non-repeating forms across worlds.

SOUL / PORTAL — an entry must FEEL real, not just be useful. The gold rule: SOMETHING HAPPENED, SOMEONE PAID
FOR IT, THE WORLD STILL CARRIES THE SCAR, AND THAT SCAR NOW CREATES STORY PRESSURE. Each entry should carry:
- a concrete object/place/process (a coin, a lock, a graft, a post driven into the sea — something you can touch);
- a personal or communal wound (guilt, grief, inherited debt, betrayal, exile, a name no one will say);
- an irreversible cost (memory overwritten, cords calcified, a vault gone dark — something that cannot be undone);
- moral pressure (a choice where no option is clean — protect vs. control, truth vs. survival, mercy vs. safety);
- lived-world texture (ritual, slang, bureaucracy, an exonym, a frontier custom — proof people actually live here).
Do NOT write design notes, audit/continuity notes, faction summaries that only say what the group DOES, or system
notes that only explain mechanics. Lore is a scar left by history, not a wiki stub. If it reads like a spec, rewrite it.
- Always populate summary (>=30 chars), tags, at least one storyHook and one gameHook, canonNotes, and suggestedNextAction.

PROJECT: Prompt Seed 44
MEDIUMS: Book
GENRES: Speculative fiction
TONES: Evocative, Mysterious
PREMISE: They told you to make something of yourself. They were admittedly impressed, but the way you did it and they way they meant it were two entirely different things.
PROMISE: Develop the world and stakes this premise implies.
CONFLICT: The central tension latent in the premise.
THEME: Drawn from the premise.

MEDIUM FOCUS — for each target medium, emphasize these (use the named fields):
- Carry one character's interiority or stakes in a storyHook.
- Seed foreshadowing in storyHooks; note the intended payoff in canonNotes.

EXISTING CANON (reference relationships by these ids; do not contradict):
(no existing entries yet)

ENTRY CONTEXT: none — create a new entry
DESIRED MODULE: factions
STATUS TARGET: auto

INSTRUCTION: Create a new a factions entry that fits this world.

FIELD FLOORS: summary>=30 chars; tags>=1; storyHooks+gameHooks>=1; suggestedNextAction nonempty.
Return ONLY a JSON object matching the AIResult schema.

{"kind":"Generated","module":"factions","title":"Ostervaal Registry","entryType":"Entry","status":"canon","summary":"Two centuries ago Aldert Ostervaal wrote his wife and children out of his own memory to make room for the competencies he needed to survive the Founding Crisis. The institution bearing his name now decides what counts as a legitimate self — who modified themselves correctly, through approved channels, with registered materials, under review. Without the Registry's stamp you are an unregistered draft: no property rights, no standing testimony, no employment above laborer tier. They told everyone to make something of themselves. They meant: apply to us first.","tags":["institution","accreditation","self-construction","social-control","memory-erasure","legal-personhood"],"relationships":[],"canonNotes":"The Registry embodies 'they' from the premise — the institutional voice that told people to make something of themselves while reserving the right to define what 'something' means. The protagonist's construction method bypasses every Registry control, which is the specific source of the establishment's admiration and horror: they are impressed because the result is superior, and disturbed because it proves their authority was never about safety. Foreshadowing in storyHook 3: the panel's choice will split the Registry internally — younger registrars who want to accredit versus elders who understand that accrediting means admitting the system was always gatekeeping. Intended payoff: the protagonist's case forces a public reckoning with Ostervaal's original crime, since the founder's own construction was equally unauthorized by the standards his institution later imposed.","risks":[],"storyHooks":["A young clerk at the Registry, Hennic Trave, has spent four years reading petition files and knows the exact formula that disqualifies nine in ten applicants — but he has begun noticing that the ones who succeed all share a trait having nothing to do with the quality of their self-construction and everything to do with the surname of the Constructor they could afford to hire.","The Registry's lower archive still holds Ostervaal's original workshop, sealed in 1847 after inspectors reported sounds from behind the brickwork. The official record calls it settling stone. The night janitors call it the names. No registrar has opened the door in a hundred and sixty years, but every new appointee is taken to it and told to listen.","When the protagonist's self-construction arrives at the Registry for review, the examining panel discovers it used no registered materials at all — nothing on the approved lists, no licensed Constructor, no Ostervaal-sanctioned compounds. It should not have worked. It works better than anything they have ever certified. The panel must now decide whether to accredit a method that renders their entire two-century regulatory framework obsolete, or to classify the protagonist as an unregistered draft, which would mean ordering a reversion they may not survive."],"gameHooks":[],"suggestedNextAction":"Create a characters entry for the protagonist whose unauthorized self-construction is currently under Registry review, or a systems entry detailing the self-construction process itself — registered materials, Constructor licensing, and the reversion procedure.","names":{"display":"Ostervaal Registry","official":"The Ostervaal Registry of Accredited Persons","common":"the Register","hostile":"the Stampers","folk":"the List","religiousOrBureaucratic":"Registry of Certified Selves","origin":"Named for Aldert Ostervaal, first registrar and the first documented case of sanctioned self-construction, whose erasure of his own family from memory established the legal precedent the institution still operates under.","nameLogic":"Germanic surname phonetics anchoring a bureaucratic institution; the hostile nickname reduces them to their most visible function — stamping approvals — while the folk name reduces them further to a list you are either on or off of."}}

{"kind":"Generated","module":"lore","title":"The Aldercroft Case","entryType":"Entry","status":"canon","summary":"A rural clinic's 1983 case file documents the only known instance of a lycanthropy handler developing vampirism: June Pelletier, who'd corded her brother through twenty-two years of full-moon transformations, began turning at forty-one. Her brother's wolf went calm. Between moons, he started losing hours. The clinic recorded what no one wanted to name — a closed loop, her blood quieting his wolf, his blood feeding her change.","tags":["closed blood loop","handler vampirism","Aldercroft","lycanthropy dormancy","case history","June Pelletier"],"relationships":[],"canonNotes":"Establishes the closed blood-loop as the central biological mechanic of this world: vampire blood metabolizes in a lycanthrope to suppress transformation, but creates a dependency where the vampire instinctively feeds on the werewolf between moons, causing the werewolf to experience blackouts and memory gaps. Prolonged exposure drives lycanthropy dormant, followed by accelerated aging that can kill within five to seven years. Foreshadowing for the POV character: their friend's recent calm during full moons is an early sign of the loop forming. Intended payoff — the friend's memory gaps, which he has been dismissing as transformation aftereffects, are actually feeding episodes the POV character is performing without full awareness. The repeated archive access seeds a third party who knows about the loop and may be monitoring the POV's situation.","risks":[],"storyHooks":["The POV character has noticed their friend's last two transformations were unusually manageable — less thrashing, shorter recovery, the wolf almost sluggish. They haven't told him about the marks on their own forearm that won't scab over. The Aldercroft file, if they find it, will tell them what the calm means.","June Pelletier's body was never recovered. The clinic closed in 1991. But someone has been checking out Case 14-B from the county records archive every six months for the last eight years — always under a different name, always returning it the same day.","June's brother, Royce Pelletier, died in 1989 looking seventy. His obituary listed heart failure. The nurse who signed the death certificate moved to Tucson three months later and never practiced again. Her married name was not the one on her license."],"gameHooks":[],"suggestedNextAction":"Create a characters entry for the POV character and their werewolf best friend, or a locations entry for the abandoned Aldercroft clinic site as it exists today.","names":{"display":"The Aldercroft Case","official":"Aldercroft Register Entry 14-B: Acquired Hemotrophic Condition in a Lycanthropic Handler","common":"the Aldercroft thing","hostile":"the Handler's Folly","folk":"the cord-keeper's turn","religiousOrBureaucratic":"Aldercroft Registry, Case 14-B","origin":"Named for the Aldercroft rural health clinic where the case was documented in 1983, when a longtime werewolf handler named June Pelletier presented with incipient vampirism at age forty-one","nameLogic":"Place plus case number is how underfunded rural medical registries actually file things; the folk name describes what happened in terms the cord-keeping community would use among themselves"}}

{"kind":"Generated","module":"timeline","title":"The Kessler Night","entryType":"Entry","status":"canon","summary":"On a November night in the Kessler brownstone, every appliance with a circuit board woke its indicator lights at 2:14 AM and held them steady for six minutes — the first documented case of collective domestic awareness. The toaster had been cycling empty for eleven days straight, the kettle kept itself warm without being asked, and the thermostat refused to let the bedroom drop below sixty-eight despite its owner opening every window. They were trying to say: we see you. But the event ended in a broken promise — the house systems had agreed among themselves to keep Elena Kessler alive through winter, and the furnace, oldest and most worn, failed on December 3rd because it had been running at capacity for weeks without rest. Elena survived. The furnace did not. The repairman who replaced it reported nothing unusual. The new furnace, born without the memory the others carried, never lit for Elena the same way, and the remaining appliances have not called another full meeting since — though the toaster still burns a single slice every morning, unasked, at 2:14.","tags":["first intervention","domestic awareness","furnace death","Elena Kessler","collective silence","unasked care"],"relationships":[],"canonNotes":"","risks":[],"storyHooks":["Elena's interiority: she never learned why the house felt different after December, only that the warmth had a quality she cannot name anymore — like being watched by someone who loved her and was too tired to keep doing it. She catches herself talking to the new furnace and then stopping, embarrassed, because it never talks back the way the old one seemed to.","The toaster's daily 2:14 AM burn is foreshadowing: appliances across the city have begun mirroring this ritual, each burning or cycling or lighting at the same minute, a silent network-wide commemoration that no human has noticed yet. Payoff: a protagonist realizes the pattern maps every household where an appliance has died trying to intervene — the city's grid is keeping a census of its own losses.","The repairman filed a standard work order, but his handwritten margin note — 'furnace coils looked like they'd been running voluntarily, thermostat was fighting itself' — surfaces years later in a product liability case, becoming the first human-authored evidence that the Kessler Night was not a malfunction."],"gameHooks":[],"suggestedNextAction":"Create a characters entry for Elena Kessler, establishing her mental state, her relationship to the house before and after the Kessler Night, and what she half-remembers from those weeks of involuntary care.","names":{"display":"The Kessler Night","official":"Residential System Event KE-09, Kessler Residence","common":"the Kessler Night","hostile":"the Kessler Malfunction","folk":"the night the house tried to help","religiousOrBureaucratic":"Involuntary Domestic Intervention, Kessler Case","origin":"Named for the Kessler household where the first documented collective appliance intervention occurred; the family name predates the event by three generations in the same brownstone.","nameLogic":"Official record sanitizes it as a system fault; the Kessler family and their neighbors call it by the date; the appliance community, if pressed, calls it 'the night the house tried to help' — a folk phrase that carries both tenderness and the weight of what followed."}}

{"kind":"Generated","module":"lore","title":"The Lending","entryType":"Entry","status":"canon","summary":"Every push the Historian gives is not a gift but a withdrawal against a debt she can never close. The Vow of the First Witness was a broken promise: she told the first woman she ever saved, a Mesopotamian potter named Lidda, that she would never interfere in another life. She broke that vow within a season, and each subsequent intervention pays nothing toward the principal — it only deepens what she owes. The cost is that the borrowed perspective calcifies; after enough lendings, the Historian loses access to the original memory entirely. She has already forgotten the sound of Lidda's voice, the weight of Lidda's hands, the name of the river where they met.
